Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd sit down for a solid session, but then I'd get distracted or just feel too anxious to focus properly. I usually ended up cramming things into about 10 hours a week, spread out whenever I could manage it. I relied heavily on listening to lecture recordings while doing other things, which worked for my auditory learning style but probably wasn't the most efficient approach.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly I'd be logging in constantly when a deadline approached. The anxiety made it hard to maintain any kind of regular schedule because I'd avoid the work when I felt overwhelmed, which was often. It's like I knew what I should be doing but couldn't make myself do it consistently.
Q3: Usually it was panic about an upcoming deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a new discussion post or announcement, and that would prompt me to check in. Other times I'd log in just to listen to lecture recordings while I was doing chores or commuting. The guilt of falling behind was a big motivator too, even if I didn't always do much once I was there.
Q4: Yeah, there was a point mid-semester where I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS most mornings with my coffee. I think it happened because I started using the mindfulness techniques they suggested, which helped with my anxiety enough that I could face the platform without feeling overwhelmed. But that routine fell apart when assignments piled up and my stress spiked again. It was good while it lasted though.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd play them on repeat sometimes, just letting the information sink in while I did other things. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too because hearing other students' perspectives helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. I also found the peer interactions valuable when I actually engaged with them.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and PDFs. I'd download them with good intentions but then never actually read them because staring at text just doesn't work for my brain. Some of the optional practice quizzes I skipped because they felt like extra pressure I couldn't handle. I know I probably should have used them more, but my anxiety made anything 'extra' feel impossible.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could tell it would directly help me understand something I was confused about or if it counted toward my grade. I also valued things I could engage with passively, like listening to discussions or lectures. If something required too much active effort when I was already stressed, it just felt like another burden even if it was probably useful.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Required ones I had to do regardless of how I felt, so there was no choice involved. But optional ones meant I had to decide whether to add more to my plate, and with my anxiety levels, that decision usually ended with me avoiding them. There was always this guilt about not doing the optional stuff, which made me feel worse about myself.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost all the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone constantly, even when I wasn't near my computer. I'd be on the bus, at the gym, doing dishes, all while replaying lecture content. I also talked through concepts out loud to myself, which sounds weird but really helped me process information. None of that would show up anywhere.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be going over material in my head, talking through problems out loud, or listening to downloaded lectures on repeat. The LMS would show nothing for those days. I also did a lot of studying by explaining concepts to my roommate, which helped me understand things better but obviously left no digital trace. My best learning often happened away from the screen.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the audio, and then listen to it maybe five or six more times offline. My exam scores were decent because of all that repetition, but the system only saw that single initial view. It's actually kind of funny how little my LMS activity reflected my actual engagement with the audio content.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially around midterms when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd basically freeze, unable to make myself do anything productive. The mindfulness intervention helped somewhat, but my resilience is pretty low so any setback felt catastrophic. When I got overwhelmed, I'd just stop logging in for days at a time.
Q13: Fatigue was huge for me. Being 28 and dealing with high stress meant I was often just exhausted. When I was tired, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at it without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely affected things too, though I don't have extracurriculars, just life stuff that piles up. Confusion about assignments sometimes paralyzed me completely.
Q14: Oh, definitely. There were times I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through content, maybe even watching a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be so anxious about everything I needed to do that I couldn't actually focus on what was in front of me. Those sessions felt like going through the motions without any real learning happening.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible, like there was this wall between me and the work. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but that better feeling rarely came. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this, even though I understood the material well enough to do okay on exams.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the motivation I'd been lacking all week. My LMS activity probably looked like nothing, nothing, nothing, then a huge spike right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure was the only thing that could cut through my avoidance.
Q17: Feedback actually helped when it was positive because it gave me a small boost of confidence. But negative or critical feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse and caused me to avoid that area of the course for a while. I tried to use feedback constructively, but my low resilience meant setbacks hit me harder than they probably should have.
Q18: The course structure was decent, but I think I would have benefited from more frequent smaller deadlines instead of a few big ones. The big deadlines let me procrastinate too much. Having discussion requirements actually helped because it forced me to engage with peers, which I might not have done otherwise. The flexibility was both good and bad for someone like me.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement much easier since I could learn by listening. But the amount of reading materials was harder for me to engage with given my learning style. I appreciated that they offered the mindfulness intervention, which helped with my anxiety somewhat. Having clear expectations upfront would have reduced some of my stress about not knowing what was coming.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on a bad week, honestly. You might log in a lot because you're anxious and checking things, but the anxiety prevents you from actually completing and submitting work. It's like being stuck in a loop of looking at what you need to do without being able to do it. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. My exam scores were pretty good because I did a lot of learning offline through audio and repetition. Someone could study effectively using downloaded materials or their own notes and barely touch the LMS but still understand the content well. The platform only captures one dimension of how people learn.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active and whether you're meeting deadlines. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, whether someone is actually absorbing material or just going through motions. They miss offline studying, audio learning, verbal processing, and all the mental work that happens away from a screen. They also can't capture emotional states that affect learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. For me, it's when I'm genuinely curious about a concept and want to explore it, even if that exploration happens while I'm doing dishes and listening to a lecture. It's also about feeling connected to the course community, even minimally. Real engagement is internal, not just clicking buttons.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety throughout the course, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope better. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures or talking through concepts out loud. It also wouldn't show that despite my scattered engagement patterns, I genuinely cared about learning the material. My emotional journey through this course is completely invisible in the data.
